coredata.dc:title: Effect of AlN dimers on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dot: DFT investigations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ajeel, Fouad N.
Khamees, Salam K.
Mohsin, Kareem H.
Mutier, Mohammed N.
coredata.dc:description: Tuning the electronic properties of engineered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is used to produce nanoelectronic devices, which are important in many applications. Chemical doping is applied to give GQDs controllable electronic properties. As a model for GQDs, we considered a molecule with 7 benzene rings and a hydrogen atom at the end of each edge carbon atom (written as C24H12). We considered engineering electronic characteristics in a GQD by altering its geometrical pattern of aluminium nitride (AlN) dimers, and so we employed the density function theory (DFT) approach with B3LYP/6-31G level to study the GQD with and without different dopants of AlN dimers. Ortho, meta, and para position dimers of aluminum and nitrogen atoms are used in the making of the three types of AlN dimers. The electronic properties which including DOS resolution, total energies, HOMO and LUMO energies, Fermi level, bandgap, reactivity descriptors, and molecular electrostatic potential are considered. It has been determined that the presence of AlN dimers has a major impact on the bandgap of the GQD. Based on our findings, the electronic characteristics of GQD are affected not only by the number of GQD dimers present but also by the arrangement of AlN dimers within the GQD. By suitably doping AlN dimers, we can tune the bandgap of GQDs. These results contribute to a better understanding of how chemical doping modifies electronic properties of GQDs and inspires the development of nanodevices with enhanced electronic performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
AlN dimers
Electronic properties
DFT
Bandgap

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Pure graphene quantum dot structure, and their (b) density of state, (c) structured geometries and their the bond lengths, (d) molecular electrostatic potential graphic considered in the gas phase.
The structures of graphene quantum dot with AlN dimers forming (a) AlN-1, (b) AlN-2, (c) AlN-3, and (d) AlN-4 structures with their density of state.
The structured geometries and the bond lengths in angstrom unit of graphene quantum dot with AlN dimers forming (a) AlN-1, (b) AlN-2, (c) AlN-3, and (d) AlN-4 structures.

The molecular electrostatic potential graphic calculated in the gas phase of graphene quantum dot with AlN dimers forming (a) AlN-1, (b) AlN-2, (c) AlN-3, and (d) AlN-4 structures. A spectrum of different colors, with red having the lowest electrostatic potential energy values and blue having the greatest.
The type and number of each element and total number of atoms for each structure under study.

row.entry: Total number of atoms for each structure
(AlN-X)-codoped GQD where X=1, 2, 3, and 4 which denote ortho, meta, para, and ortho with distance forms.

head.ce:title: Effect of AlN dimers on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dot: DFT investigations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, University of Sfax, Tunisia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Physics, College of Science, University of Sfax, Tunisia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, University of Sumer, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, College of Science, University of Sumer, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Directorate of Education, Ministry of Education, Thi-qar, Iraq

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, College of Science, University of Sfax, Tunisia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tuning the electronic properties of engineered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is used to produce nanoelectronic devices, which are important in many applications. Chemical doping is applied to give GQDs controllable electronic properties. As a model for GQDs, we considered a molecule with 7 benzene rings and a hydrogen atom at the end of each edge carbon atom (written as C24H12). We considered engineering electronic characteristics in a GQD by altering its geometrical pattern of aluminium nitride (AlN) dimers, and so we employed the density function theory (DFT) approach with B3LYP/6-31G level to study the GQD with and without different dopants of AlN dimers. Ortho, meta, and para position dimers of aluminum and nitrogen atoms are used in the making of the three types of AlN dimers. The electronic properties which including DOS resolution, total energies, HOMO and LUMO energies, Fermi level, bandgap, reactivity descriptors, and molecular electrostatic potential are considered. It has been determined that the presence of AlN dimers has a major impact on the bandgap of the GQD. Based on our findings, the electronic characteristics of GQD are affected not only by the number of GQD dimers present but also by the arrangement of AlN dimers within the GQD. By suitably doping AlN dimers, we can tune the bandgap of GQDs. These results contribute to a better understanding of how chemical doping modifies electronic properties of GQDs and inspires the development of nanodevices with enhanced electronic performance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most researched nanoscale materials is two-dimensional (2D) graphene because of its intriguing electronic and optical properties [1,2]. Graphene sheets have a significant amount of promise in the field of technology as a result of their capacity to be doped, functionalized, and self-organized [3,4]. In 2004, graphene was extracted via the process of mechanical exfoliation. Graphene is a fully 2D structure that is atomically thin and has carbon atoms organized in a honeycomb configuration [1,5]. Because of its unique electronic band structure, which is distinguished by the massless Dirac spectrum, it has special physical characteristics [6–8]. Graphene is used in a wide number of applications in the fields of electronics, spintronics, photonics, and optoelectronics because it has several unique physical features [2,9-11]. However, graphene not having an energy gap prevents it from being used as a semiconductor material, which limits its potential applications [12–15]. Several techniques based on structural modifications have been proposed to improve graphene's electronic characteristics. By reducing a graphene channel to 100 nm in width, one may use the geometrical effect or quantum confinement, which alters the band gap by orders of magnitude [1,15]. Graphene quantum dots are nanoclusters of graphene that have charge carriers confined in all three directions. They are a possible contender that has the potential to expand the use of graphene as a semiconductor material [2,16-18].

Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have lately attracted a lot of interest because of their unusual electrical and optoelectronic characteristics and potential uses in fields such as nanoelectronics, nanomedicine, photovoltaics, supercapacitors, and biosensors [19,20]. The quantum confinement effect causes a band gap to shift and a high photoluminescence response in GQDs, making them a fascinating alternative to graphene [21–23]. It has been shown that the band gap increases when the size of GQDs decreases [24]. The enhanced doping feasibility is another intriguing property, since it is useful in sensing and electrocatalysis applications [15,25,26]. Most methods used to create GQDs via experimentation result in the creation of various chemical functional groups, most notably near the edges but also in the basal plane [18,19]. However, recent research has shown that functionalizing GQDs and doping them with other atoms can result in excellent tuning of their optoelectronic properties [18,25,27]. The improved electronic properties and biological activity of the functionalizing GQDs provide new possibilities for their use in various applications [8,15,28-31].

This study uses first-principles DFT calculations to look into how AlN dimers change the structure and electronic features of graphene quantum dots. In order to determine the most stable structure, we identify three forms of AlN dimers: ortho, meta, and para. One strategy for achieving desired electronic properties via engineering is the introduction of substitutional impurities. AlN dimers may therefore be used to adjust the electronic properties of GQDs. Small shifts in the bandgap may change the electronic conductivity, which plays a crucial role in the development of graphene quantum dots for applications in nanoelectronics and solar cells.

The wide range of uses of GQDs as a material has been attributed to the ability to design and manipulate their properties through the control of their shape and components. Within the framework of the DFT, the chemical reactivity parameters, which include chemical hardness η , chemical potential μ , and the electrophilicity index ω , are very helpful for studying reactivity trend, excited states, and toxicity analysis [42,43]. The definitions of chemical potential and chemical hardness can be correspondingly expressed as follows [2,44,45]:
(1)
η =
(
I P − E A
)
2
(2)
μ = −
(
I P + E A
)
2
(3)
ω =
μ
2
2 η

Koopmans theorem can be utilized to estimate the ionization potential (
I P
) and electron affinity (
E A
) [46–48]:
(4)
I P = −
E
HOMO
(5)
E A = −
E LUMO
where
E
H O M O
and
E
L U M O
are the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO.

In addition, we determine the electronic band gap and the Fermi level energy using the following formula [2,49]:
(6)
E g
=
E LUMO
−
E
HOMO
(7)
E FL
=
(
E
HOMO
+
E LUMO
)
2

To investigate the structural stability of the AlN-codoped GQDs, the formation energies
E F
for all structures were also calculated [50]:
(8)
E f
=
E
AlN −
codoped
GQD
−
E
GQD
− x
(
E
Al
+
E
N
)
− 2
E C
here,
E
AlN −
codoped
GQD
and
E
GQD
are the total energy of the optimized structure of AlN-codoped GQDs and pure GQD respectively and ‘ x ’ is the number of dopant Al and N atoms.
E
Al
,
E
N
and
E C
is the total energy of dopant (Al and N) atoms and carbon atom respectively. The most stability of the doped structures belongs to the most negative
E F
value.

Pure graphene quantum dots, as seen in Fig. 1 (a), contain a 7-benzene ring with an average CC bond length of around 1.42, which is consistent with the previously published values [35,45,51,52]. As shown in Fig. 1(a), pure graphene quantum dots consist of a 7-benzene ring with an average CC bond length of 1.42 Å, which is consistent with previous studies [35,45,51,52]. To achieve an in-depth understanding of the alterations in electronic properties of pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs), it is imperative to perform an analysis of the electronic density of states (DOS) of GQDs both pre- and post-substitution of carbon atoms with AlN dimers.

The results presented in Fig. 1(b) illustrate the DOS analysis of pure GQD, including the energy difference for the HOMO and LUMO, as well as the energy gap (
E g
= 4.08 eV). The observed energy gap value exhibits characteristics of a semiconductor material with a large bandgap, which is consistent with the reference [34,35,45,53]. The DOS analysis, which includes the energy difference for the HOMO, LUMO, and pure GQD's energy gap (
E g
= 4.08 eV), is shown in Fig. 1(b). This value of the energy gap showing a semiconductor behavior with big bandgap, and it are in agreement with the reference [34,35,45,53]. The frequency of the imaginary harmonic is also zero. Such frequencies show that the structure has reached a true stationary state on the surface of the potential energy.

The present study aims to examine the effect of introducing AlN dimer at different positions on the electronic characteristics of GQD. As shown in Fig. 2 , we assume four different types of AlN dimers, that shows (a) Al and N atoms are doped at ortho position (AlN-1), (b) Al or N atoms are placed adjacent to one another in meta position (AlN-2), (c) Al and N are at para position (AlN-3), and (d) AlN pair is at ortho position but distance between two AlN pairs is large (AlN-4).

It has been observed that the introduction of AlN impurities causes a shift in the electronic DOS as well as the band gap that exists between the valence and conduction bands. It was also found that shifting the position of the AlN impurities while keeping the concentrations the same has an effect on the DOS diagrams as well as the electronic band gap (
E g
). For instance, the
E g
changes from −27.82% to −48.09% when the location of Al and N atoms in GQD structures is altered, and this is significant in a number of contexts. By doping GQD structures with AlN dimers, the energy gap may be lowered, turning GQD into a semiconductor with small bandgap.

The bond energies of the CC, C-Al, CN, and Al-N bonds in a GQD also contribute to the general structural stability of the GQD. Based on these findings, shown in Fig. 3 , we may rank the binding energies from most energetic to least: AL-N > CC > NC > Al-C. Therefore, the most stable structure will have a large number of Al-N bonds and fewer Al-Al and NN bonds. The bandgap and formation energy of all GQDs are shown in Table 2 using the DFT approach to verify their structural stability. The AlN-4 structure is the most stable of the four AlN-structures because its value of
E F
is the highest when compared to the values of the other structures. Our DFT analysis also reveals that the AlN-2 structure has the lowest formation energy, making it the most unstable. Consequently, the AlN ortho dimer with a large distance is the most stable structure, as shown by the formation energy given in Table 2.

Bond lengths are altered and multiple bonds have been identified as a result of the substitutional doping of Al and N atoms in GQDs. It is possible to rank the average bond length from longest to shortest as follows: Al-C > Al-N > CC > CN. Sublattice symmetry in AlN-codoped GQDs will be broken due to distortion brought on by these varying bond lengths. As a result, at high concentration ratios of AlN dimers, it brings to significant alteration in electronic properties of GQDs.

Additionally, Table 2 presented a summary of the calculated data for the energetic state include: the HOMO energies (
E HOMO
,
eV
), Fermi level energy (
E FL
,
eV
), LUMO energies (
E LUMO
,
eV
), band gap (
E g
,
eV
), change of
E g
(
Δ
E g
) and formation energies (
E F
) for graphene quantum dot with and without AlN dimers. According to the results of the frontier molecular orbital study, the HOMO and LUMO energies of the pure GQD are −5.487 and −1.403 eV These values slowly rise when AlN dimers are added. As can be seen in Table 2, not only is the
E g
modified, but also the Fermi level energy (
E FL
). This effect also causes a rise in the work function, which is important in field emission applications.

Furthermore, we created a counter map of the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) and determined the total electron density. The use of visualizations that represent variable charge distributions offers helpful insights into intermolecular interactions, the reactive positioning of molecules, molecular properties, and the ability to predict electrophilic and nucleophilic reactions at the site of doping [54]. Fig. 4 displays the revealed findings for energy surfaces of the MEP of graphene quantum dots with and without AlN dimers. The difference in electronegativity between the two atoms in a chemical bond is another essential parameter that helps characterize the nature of the interaction. A spectrum of colors is used to illustrate the electrostatic potential.

The reactivity descriptors that are shown in Table 3 are evaluated with the use of Koopman's theorem. These properties include the ionization potential, electron affinity, electronegativity, chemical hardness, chemical softness, and electrophilicity, all of which are highly helpful for studying reactivity trends, and excited states, and assessing toxicity [55,56]. According to Koopman's theorem, the HOMO energy approximates the negative experimental ionization potential, while the LUMO energy represents the negative electron affinity [57,58]. One of the most helpful indicators of a molecule's stability and reactivity is its chemical hardness ( η ) of the system. It is expected that molecules with the lowest possible values for chemical hardness will behave as effective inhibitors of corrosion, as seen for meta position for (AlN-2)-codoped GQD.

Finally, in order to take into account how AlN dimers affect the electronic characteristics of GQDs, we must consider the equation
σ ∝ exp (
−
E g
/ 2
k β
T
)
, which implies that a small change in
E g
can alter the electrical conductivity of the GQD [59]. Where
σ ,
k β
,
and
T
are the electric conductivity, the Boltzmann's constant, and the temperature 298.14 K. The equation showed, lower values of
E g
at a given temperature result in higher levels of electric conductivity. As a direct result of this, there is a significant shift in the electrical conductivity of the system when AlN dimers are present in pure GQD. Thus, we can be modifying the electronic properties of GQDs through the addition of AlN dimers.

To summarize, we use density functional theory calculations to study the electronic ground state of pure and AlN-doped graphene quantum dots. The results demonstrate that the electronic structure of GQD is influenced by the location of AlN dimers. It has been demonstrated that the antiaromatic structure of the meta AlN dimers is responsible for the low bandgap of the GQD. In contrast, both ortho and para AlN dimers have a relatively small bandgap, with the para AlN dimer structure having a smaller bandgap than the ortho AlN structure. It has also been shown that the global chemical indices of GQD are affected by the presence of AlN dimers. By raising the energy of the HOMO, the Fermi level, and the LUMO, the HOMO-LUMO gap is significantly changed. This modification is essential in numerous applications because electrical conductivity can be changed by even a small bandgap shift. Finally, we are able to alter the electronic properties of GQDs by introducing AlN dimers in various positions.

All authors participated in the idea and design of this study. Fouad N. Ajeel achieved supervision, computations, and data curation. Salam K. Khamees and Mohammed N. Mutier verified the analytical methods of this work. Kareem H. Mohsin wrote the original draft of the paper. All authors discussed the results and contributed to writing, review, and editing the final paper.
No data were utilized in the analysis presented in the article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no conflict of interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: There was no specific funding for this work from any governmental, commercial, or non-profit organization.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical insights into tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots through ZnO doping

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.N. Ajeel, K.H. Mohsin, H.G. Shakier, S.K. Khamees, and M.N. Mutier, "Theoretical insights into tunable electronic properties of graphene quantum dots through ZnO doping," Chem. Phys. Impact, vol. 7, p. 100305, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement the electronic and optical properties of the graphene nanoflakes in the present S impurities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Khudhair, M.H. Mohammed, F.N. Ajeel, and S.H. Mohammed, "Enhancement the electronic and optical properties of the graphene nanoflakes in the present S impurities," Chem. Phys. Impact, vol. 6, p. 100154, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning the structural, electronic, and optical properties of monolayer graphene through heteroatom doping: a first-principles study with future light sensing applications

F. Schwierz, "Graphene transistors," Nat. Nanotechnol., vol. 5, no. 7, pp. 487–496, 2010.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Raissi et al., "The effects of substitutions on structure, electron density, resonance and intramolecular hydrogen bonding strength in 3-mercapto-propenethial," J. Mol. Struct. THEOCHEM, vol. 960, no. 1–3, pp. 1–9, 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supramolecular assembly of edge functionalized top-down chiral graphene quantum dots
